coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient dehydrogenation of hydrazine borane over CoIr/TiO2 catalyst
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ye
Kang, Xia
Liu, Dong-Xue
Chen, Ze-Yu
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, hydrazine borane (HB) as an excellent hydrogen material has been extensively studied by researchers because of its substantial hydrogen content (15.4 wt%), favourable chemical stability, eco-friendliness and being easy to synthesize. With Higher activity, catalysts of HB dehydrogenation will undoubtedly be more desirable for practical applications. In this work, CoIr nanoparticles (NPs) are successfully immobilized on TiO2 substrate, achieving outstanding catalytic performance and 100% conversion for HB dehydrogenation. Particularly, Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 can complete the reaction for HB dehydrogenation at 323 K within 32 s (0.53 min), and shows a fairly high turnover frequency (TOF) value (5625 h−1), which is higher than the values achieved by most Ni-based catalysts reported so far in the same condition. This superior catalytic performance can be attributed to uniform dispersion of metal NPs with small size and strong interaction among the CoIr NPs and the substrate. It is unquestionable that our work will help to promote the use of HB as a promising hydrogen storage material for fuel cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrazine borane
Dehydrogenation
CoIr nanoparticles
High efficiency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD diffraction patterns of Co0·6Ir0·4/TiO2, Co0·6Ir0·4/TiO2-c (Co0·6Ir0·4/TiO2 annealed in Ar atmosphere at 873 K) and TiO2. XPS spectrum of (b) Co 2p and (c) Ir 4f for Co0·6Ir0.4, Co/TiO2, and Co0·6Ir0·4/TiO2.
(a) TEM image, (b) HAADF-STEM image and STEM-EDX elemental mapping for (c) Co, (d) Ir, (e) O, and (f) Ti elements of Co0·6Ir0·4/TiO2.

(a) Process plots of the molar ratio of n(H2+N2)/n(N2H4BH3) versus time for HB (0.5 M, 2 mL) decomposition catalyzed by TiO2, Co0·6Ir0.4, Ir/TiO2, Co/TiO2 and Co0·6Ir0·4/TiO2. (b) The conversion and TOF for the same amount of HB decomposition catalyzed by CoxIr(1-x)/TiO2.

(a) Time-course plots for the decomposition of HB (two steps divided by dashed lines) catalyzed by Co0·6Ir0·4/TiO2 in a temperature range from 303 K to 333 K, and (b) its related Arrhenius plots. (c) Recycle test for H2 generation from the aqueous solution of HB over Co0·6Ir0·4/TiO2 at 323 K.
Activities in terms of TOF values of different catalysts for hydrogen generation from HB aqueous solution.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient dehydrogenation of hydrazine borane over CoIr/TiO2 catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials, Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun, 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials, Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun, 130022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, hydrazine borane (HB) as an excellent hydrogen material has been extensively studied by researchers because of its substantial hydrogen content (15.4 wt%), favourable chemical stability, eco-friendliness and being easy to synthesize. With Higher activity, catalysts of HB dehydrogenation will undoubtedly be more desirable for practical applications. In this work, CoIr nanoparticles (NPs) are successfully immobilized on TiO2 substrate, achieving outstanding catalytic performance and 100% conversion for HB dehydrogenation. Particularly, Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 can complete the reaction for HB dehydrogenation at 323 K within 32 s (0.53 min), and shows a fairly high turnover frequency (TOF) value (5625 h−1), which is higher than the values achieved by most Ni-based catalysts reported so far in the same condition. This superior catalytic performance can be attributed to uniform dispersion of metal NPs with small size and strong interaction among the CoIr NPs and the substrate. It is unquestionable that our work will help to promote the use of HB as a promising hydrogen storage material for fuel cells.

Hydrazine borane (N2H4BH3) is regarded as a promising hydrogen storage material due to its high hydrogen content (15.4 wt%). Its dehydrogenation conversion and rate are closely related to the selected catalyst. The synthesized Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 can catalyze the complete hydrolysis of HB within 32s with a TOF as high as 5625 h−1 at 323 K.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2), a green, renewable and clean energy source, has received widespread attention to overcome the worsening energy crisis and serious environmental pollution caused by fossil fuel consumption [1–4]. Currently, the methods for hydrogen production mainly include electrocatalytic water splitting [5–10], photocatalytic water splitting [11–13], and catalysing hydrogen storage materials (NaBH4, NH3BH3, etc.) decomposition [14–19]. Owing to their huge advantages such as superior safety, convenience, and high efficiency, hydrogen storage materials have great potential for large-scale practical applications [20–23]. However, how to realize safe and efficient hydrogen generation/storage is still a huge problem in practical application [24–27]. Among all the hydrogen generation/storage materials, HB with 15.4 wt% high hydrogen content, regarded as one of the promising candidates, is easy synthesized, product green, and chemically stable [28–30]. The complete dehydrogenation of HB is obtained by two steps: the hydrolysis of BH3 group (N2H4BH3 + 3H2O → N2H4 + B(OH)3 + 3H2) and the decomposition of another group N2H4 (N2H4 → N2 + 2H2) [31,32]. The decomposition of N2H4 may also produce NH3 (3N2H4 → 4NH3 + N2), which should be avoided to maximize the dehydrogenation of N2H4BH3 [33–35]. Heterogeneous catalysts reported recently, such as Ni-alloyed noble metals (Ir, Rh, Pt, and Pd), can achieve 100% conversion, while unsatisfied performance for HB dehydrogenation limits their further application [36–41]. It is very important and urgent to design a highly efficient catalyst to take advantage of this high hydrogen content material. Co element has a good performance in HER, and has been used in the catalytic hydrogen production from boron ammonia [42,43]. The CoIr nanoparticles combined with Co and Ir have not been introduced in catalysing HB dehydrogenation. Common support materials such as TiO2, GO and g-C3N4 have attracted much attention, due to their unique properties [44–46]. TiO2 with low price and stable chemical properties is more promising in practical applications [47]. The original basic particles of the selected TiO2 (P25) have small average particle size, low density and large specific surface area [48]. In addition, TiO2 (P25) belongs to the mixed crystal type, so there are many defects in the TiO2 lattice, which may improve the catalytic performance [49].

Herein, we report a Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 nanocatalyst synthesized through a co-reduction process for dehydrogenation from HB. Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 exerts an excellent activity of 100% conversion and a remarkable TOF value of 5625 h−1 for HB dehydrogenation in alkaline solution at 323 K, exceeding almost all the reported heterogeneous catalysts before.

Hydrazine hemisulfate salt (N2H4.1/2H2SO4, Sigma-Aldrich, 98%), n-pentane (C5H12, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, ≥99%), 1,4-dioxane (C4H8O2, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, ≥99.5%), cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2.6H2O, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, 98%), iridium chloride hydrate (IrCl3, Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd, 99.8%), Titanium (IV) oxide (TiO2, type P25, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, analytically pure), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, 96%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd, 96%), hydrogen chloride (HCl, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, 37%). All chemical reagents were used as received without further purification.

HB was synthesized in accordance with the reported literature [50]. Typically, N2H4.1/2H2SO4 (21.42 g) and NaBH4 (10 g) were added into anhydrous dioxane (80 mL) with stirring for 48 h under an Ar atmosphere. The liquid after centrifugation of the precursor solution was dried in a vacuum oven at 313 K overnight, washed with n-pentane, and then dried again to obtain HB. The purity of the synthesized HB is 98.6%.

For synthesis of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2, TiO2 (20 mg) was firstly dispersed in H2O (6 mL) by sonicating treatment. Subsequently, 0.1 M of CoCl2.6H2O (600 μL) and IrCl3 (400 μL) aqueous solutions were added with magnetic stirring for 1 min. Finally, 30 mg of NaBH4 and 206 mg of NaOH were dissolved in 1 mL of water, and added under stirring for 3 min to obtain Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 hybrid. And the Co0.6Ir0.4 was also obtained with the absence of TiO2.Other CoxIr(1-x)/TiO2 specimens (x is from 0.1 to 0.9) were synthesized through the same method.

For synthesis of Co/TiO2, TiO2 (20 mg) was firstly dispersed in H2O (6 mL) by sonicating treatment. Subsequently, CoCl2.6H2O (0.1 M, 1 mL) aqueous solution was added to the treated TiO2 aqueous solution with magnetic stirring for 1 min. Then, 1.0 mL aqueous solution containing NaBH4 (30 mg) and NaOH (206 mg) was added into the above mixture under stirring for 3 min to obtain Co/TiO2 hybrid. For synthesis of Ir/TiO2 hybrids were similar to Co/TiO2, except that CoCl2.6H2O was replaced by IrCl3.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on a Rigaku D/max-2500 X-ray generator with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å). Transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai F20, Philips) and corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) were applied to analyze microstructures and compositions of the samples, and the amorphous carbon coated copper grids were used as the sample supporters. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were acquired with an ESCALABMKLL (Vacuum Generators) spectrometer using Al Kα X-rays.

Both synthesis and reaction processes take place in a two-necked flask with no loss of metals. During the HB dehydrogenation reaction, one neck of the flask was connected to a separatory funnel containing HB (0.5 M, 2 mL), the other was connected to a gas scrubber filled with HCl (0.1 M, 50 mL) to eliminate the influence of ammonia that may be generated by side reactions. A gas burette to measure the volume of the reaction gas was finally connected to this scrubber. Before the reaction started, the reactor was added with solid NaOH to adjust the pH to ensure the reaction condition of NaOH 4 M. The reaction was carried out at 323 K. For the recycle test, after the complete decomposition of HB, the mixed solution of HB (1 mmol) in NaOH (4 M, 2 mL) (ensuring the reaction condition of NaOH 4 M) is quickly added to the reactor for the next reaction. The recycle test was carried out five times under the same condition.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2, TiO2 and Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2-c (Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 after annealing at 823 K for 3 h with Ar as protective gas) are shown in Fig. 1 a. There are two different phases of TiO2 (PDF#21–1275 and PDF#21–1236) can be seen for the three specimens. For Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2, except for the diffraction peaks of TiO2, a broad peak at 40–45° is observed, which might be attributed to the formation of amorphous/low-crystalline CoIr alloy, which is also in line with the unsupported Co0.6Ir0.4 (Fig. S1). After the heat treatment, the broad peak becomes sharp and is located between the Co (111) and Ir (111), and another new peak between Co (220) and Ir (220) can be seen clearly, which further proves the formation of amorphous/low-crystalline CoIr alloy structure in Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 [51]. Similarly, as shown in Fig. S2, there are no other diffraction peaks except for TiO2 in Co/TiO2 and Ir/TiO2, while after annealing treatment under the same conditions (Co/TiO2-c and Ir/TiO2-c), the diffraction peak belongs to Co (PDF#15–0806) or Ir (PDF#46–1044) can be seen clearly [52,53], proving the amorphous/low-crystalline of Co and Ir nanoparticles on TiO2, respectively. The XRD results of annealed part of CoxIr(1-x)/TiO2 under the same conditions as above show that the main peak belonging to the CoIr alloy is closer to the diffraction peak of Ir (111) with the increase of Ir proportion (Fig. S3), which is caused by the binary alloy effect.

The chemical valence and electronic states of Co and Ir in Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2, Co/TiO2 and Ir/TiO2 and Co0.6Ir0.4 specimens are characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) after Ar gas sputtering. As shown in Fig. 1b, the binding energies (BE) of Co 2p1/2 (794.4eV) and Co 2p3/2 (778.2 eV) for Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 are attributed to metallic Co0 [54]. And the BE of Ir 4f5/2 (63.4 eV) and 4f7/2 (60.4 eV) for Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 are corresponding to metallic Ir0 (Fig. 1c) [55]. Obviously, compared to pure Co0.6Ir0.4, the XPS spectrum for Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 shows that BE of Co and Ir are shifted to the lower positions, especially for Ir, which reveals that the electrons are transferred from TiO2 (eletron donor) to CoIr nanoparticles (Fig. 1b–c). On the other hand, comparing Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 with Co/TiO2 and Ir/TiO2, it is found that the peaks of Co 2p shift to the higher BE (Fig. 1b), and the peaks of Ir 4f shift to the lower direction (Fig. 1c), proving the electron transferring from Co to Ir in Co0.6Ir0.4 alloy, which is consistent with the lower electronegativity of Co (1.88) than Ir (2.2) [56,57]. Thus, the electron donating effect of the TiO2 substrate and the electron transfer between Co and Ir lead to good synergistic effect, which is beneficial to the catalytic performance of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 for HB decomposition [32].

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 (Fig. 2 a) demonstrates that CoIr NPs with a 7.0 nm average nanoparticle size are successfully supported on TiO2 (Fig. S4). The good dispersibility and smaller particle size of CoIr NPs are attributed to the carrier role of TiO2. The high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 catalyst and its corresponding elemental mappings of Co, Ir, O, and Ti are shown in Fig. 2b–f. As can be seen, Co and Ir elements appear in nearly the same position as the substrates, which helps to prove the existence of CoIr alloy. In addition, no lattice fringes belonging to CoIr alloy nanoparticles are observed in the HRTEM images of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 (Fig. S5), further proving the amorphous/low-crystalline structure of Co0.6Ir0.4, which consists with the preceding XRD result. The amorphous/low-crystalline and small nanoparticle size of Co0.6Ir0.4 could provide more active sites, and further speed up the HB decomposition reaction.

The catalytic performance of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 for HB dehydrogenation at different temperatures (from 303 K to 333 K) are also tested (Fig. 4 a). It is clear that the catalytic performance increases with the rise of temperature. The reaction of HB dehydrogenation catalyzed by Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 can complete in 2.67, 1.13, 0.53, and 0.25 min at 303, 313, 323, and 333 K with 100% conversion, meanwhile the TOF values becoming 1286, 2647, 5625, and 12000 h−1, respectively. Based on the above results, the activation energies for BH3 group hydrolysis (Ea
1 , Part 1) of HB is calculated to be 23.21 kJ/mol, and that for N2H4 decomposition (Ea
2 , Part 2) is 70.85 kJ/mol (Fig. 4b). Visibly, the hydrolysis of BH3 group is much easier and faster than the decomposition of N2H4 [64]. Studies have shown that alkaline media can make the rate-determining step (N2H4 → N2H3∗ + H∗) proceed faster to accelerate the whole reaction [37]. To achieve this, the influence of NaOH concentration on HB dehydrogenation is shown in Fig. S8. The activity of catalyst Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 improves with the rise of NaOH concentration, and 4.0 M is the ideal concentration, indicating that the concentration of NaOH is critical to the reaction. Further increasing the concentration of NaOH could not promote the decomposition of HB, because too concentrated NaOH would affect the mass transfer process of the reaction and hinder the reaction.

The recycle stability is also one of the important factors of HB decomposition catalyst. Fig. 4c shows that, after 5 cycles, the conversion rate with catalyst of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 is still 100% at 323 K. This means that Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 has the excellent stability for HB dehydrogenation. The XRD patterns and the TEM images of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 after the reaction are shown in Fig. S9 and Fig. S10. The structure and particle size are similar with those before the reaction, demonstrating the good stability of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 for HB dehydrogenation.

In summary, we synthesized Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 by a facile co-reduction method. The catalyst Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 has good catalytic performance for HB dehydrogenation and can complete the decomposition of HB within 0.53 min at 323 K under alkaline conditions (NaOH, 4 M), corresponding the TOF value of 5625 h−1. This work shows that the CoIr catalysts have great potential for HB dehydrogenation. Meanwhile, the excellent catalytic performance of Co0.6Ir0.4/TiO2 is further beneficial to apply HB as a desirable hydrogen storage material.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author acknowledges the laboratory members who supported during the experiment and writing of the paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2022.08.252.
